source.sourcePolicy: Rules and flow are compiled only from the jurisdiction reference/source documents supplied in the Nationwide archive, plus the user-supplied Nebraska reference.

questions.prompt: Are you asking about your own record?
options: I am helping someone complete their own check

questions.prompt: Is this a state or local matter from Arizona, rather than a federal matter?
How did the Arizona case or record end?
options: Dismissed, no-billed, nolle prosequi, or not prosecuted

questions.prompt: What level or type was the charge?
Do any of these Arizona descriptions sound close to your situation?
options: None of these / I am not sure
questions.helper: This answer is optional and does not select your legal pathway. The backend uses the factual answers and state rules.

questions.prompt: Are all fines, restitution, court costs, and other required financial obligations resolved?
Do you have the Arizona criminal-history report, court docket, or certified disposition needed to verify the record?
When was the case disposed of, dismissed, completed, or discharged?
Which court or agency handled the case?
What charge, statute, or record description appears on the record?
Does the record involve any Arizona category that the source identifies as excluded or review-required?
Do you have court paperwork or a criminal-history record showing the exact charge and outcome?
Where in Arizona was the case handled?
What case, docket, cause, or arrest number appears on the record?

caseOutcomeOptions.label: Dismissed, no-billed, nolle prosequi, or not prosecuted

pathways.label: Remedy 1 — Record sealing (A.R.S. § 13-911)
pathways.summary: The broadest modern relief, effective January 1, 2023:
ruleClauses: The broadest modern relief, effective January 1, 2023:

Convictions — a person convicted of an eligible offense may seal the arrest, conviction, and sentencing records after completing all NONMONETARY terms of the sentence and the applicable waiting period (by offense class).
Dismissals / acquittals — a person whose charges were dismissed, who was acquitted, or whose charge was reduced to a lesser eligible offense may petition to seal, generally right away.
Effect — records are hidden from public view; the person may state in most contexts that the
waitingRules: Convictions — a person convicted of an eligible offense may seal the arrest, conviction, and sentencing records after completing all NONMONETARY terms of the sentence and the applicable waiting period (by offense class).

frontendBranch.entryCondition: Backend matched this jurisdiction-specific route after evaluating the cited source rules.

pathways.label: Remedy 2 — Set-aside of a conviction (A.R.S. § 13-905)
pathways.summary: • Available to most people convicted of a criminal offense, on fulfillment of the conditions of probation or sentence and discharge by the court — no waiting period. • Effect — the judgment of guilt is vacated and the person is released from penalties and disabilities, but the record stays PUBLIC with a “set aside” annotation; the conviction can still be used in a later prosecution and remains on background checks. • Firearm rights are restored on set-aside except for a serious offense (§…
ruleClauses: Available to most people convicted of a criminal offense, on fulfillment of the conditions of probation or sentence and discharge by the court — no waiting period.

Effect — the judgment of guilt is vacated and the person is released from penalties and disabilities, but the record stays PUBLIC with a “set aside” annotation; the conviction can still be used in a later prosecution and remains on background checks.
Firearm rights are restored on set-aside except for a serious offense (§ 13-706).
Certificate of Second Chance may be granted with the set-aside, easing occupational licensing.
waitingRules: Available to most people convicted of a criminal offense, on fulfillment of the conditions of probation or sentence and discharge by the court — no waiting period.

frontendBranch.entryCondition: Backend matched this jurisdiction-specific route after evaluating the cited source rules.

pathways.label: Remedy 3 — Marijuana expungement (A.R.S. § 36-2862, Prop 207)
pathways.summary: • True EXPUNGEMENT — the record is erased and accessible only to law enforcement/courts in limited situations. • Limited to Prop-207 marijuana conduct: possessing/consuming/transporting 2.5 ounces or less (≤ 12.5 g concentrate); possessing/cultivating no more than six plants at a primary residence for personal use; and associated paraphernalia. • No waiting period, and filing fees are waived; applies to arrests, charges, adjudications, and convictions.
ruleClauses: True EXPUNGEMENT — the record is erased and accessible only to law enforcement/courts in limited situations.

Limited to Prop-207 marijuana conduct: possessing/consuming/transporting 2.5 ounces or less (≤ 12.5 g concentrate); possessing/cultivating no more than six plants at a primary residence for personal use; and associated paraphernalia.
No waiting period, and filing fees are waived; applies to arrests, charges, adjudications, and convictions.
Check the marijuana path first — if the offense fits § 36-2862, expungement is the strongest, fastest, free

Otherwise test sealing (§ 13-911) and the waiting tier; use set-aside (§ 13-905) for immediate relief or when sealing isn't available. Remember the sequence strategy: many petitioners get a set-aside now and seal later once the waiting period passes.
waitingRules: No waiting period, and filing fees are waived; applies to arrests, charges, adjudications, and convictions.

Otherwise test sealing (§ 13-911) and the waiting tier; use set-aside (§ 13-905) for immediate relief or when sealing isn't available. Remember the sequence strategy: many petitioners get a set-aside now and seal later once the waiting period passes.

when.sourceConditionText: Dangerous / serious / sex / — Not eligible —
Not eligible for SEALING (§ 13-911(O))
Not eligible for SET-ASIDE (§ 13-905(K))

Convictions — a person convicted of an eligible offense may seal the arrest, conviction, and sentencing records after completing all NONMONETARY terms of the sentence and the applicable waiting period (by offense class).
Subsequent felony — a person who sealed records and later commits a felony may seal the later case only after that offense's waiting period PLUS an additional five years.
Available to most people convicted of a criminal offense, on fulfillment of the conditions of probation or sentence and discharge by the court — no waiting period.
No waiting period, and filing fees are waived; applies to arrests, charges, adjudications, and convictions.

Otherwise test sealing (§ 13-911) and the waiting tier; use set-aside (§ 13-905) for immediate relief or when sealing isn't available. Remember the sequence strategy: many petitioners get a set-aside now and seal later once the waiting period passes.
Remedy / offense level Statute Waiting period
stacks: someone who sealed an earlier record and then commits a new felony must wait that new offense's class

Only the Prop-207 conduct qualifies (≤ 2.5 oz / ≤ 6 plants / paraphernalia). A marijuana SALE offense is NOT expungeable under § 36-2862, though it may be SEALABLE under § 13-911 after the waiting period.
Remedy 1 — Record sealing (A.R.S. § 13-911)
The broadest modern relief, effective January 1, 2023:
Dismissals / acquittals — a person whose charges were dismissed, who was acquitted, or whose charge was reduced to a lesser eligible offense may petition to seal, generally right away.

Effect — records are hidden from public view; the person may state in most contexts that the arrest/conviction did not occur (with exceptions for certain sensitive positions/licenses). Law enforcement, courts, and certain agencies still have access.
Remedy 2 — Set-aside of a conviction (A.R.S. § 13-905)

Effect — the judgment of guilt is vacated and the person is released from penalties and disabilities, but the record stays PUBLIC with a “set aside” annotation; the conviction can still be used in a later prosecution and remains on background checks.
Firearm rights are restored on set-aside except for a serious offense (§ 13-706).
Certificate of Second Chance may be granted with the set-aside, easing occupational licensing.
Remedy 3 — Marijuana expungement (A.R.S. § 36-2862, Prop 207)
True EXPUNGEMENT — the record is erased and accessible only to law enforcement/courts in limited situations.

Limited to Prop-207 marijuana conduct: possessing/consuming/transporting 2.5 ounces or less (≤ 12.5 g concentrate); possessing/cultivating no more than six plants at a primary residence for personal use; and associated paraphernalia.
Check the marijuana path first — if the offense fits § 36-2862, expungement is the strongest, fastest, free
Arizona Record Relief — Agent Training Reference · LegalEase / Wilma
Marijuana ≤ 2.5 oz / ≤ 6 plants / Expungement None Erased
Dismissed / acquitted (eligible) Sealing (§ 13-911) None Hidden from public
Class 2/3 misdemeanor conv. Sealing 2 yrs Hidden from public
Class 1 misdemeanor conv. Sealing 3 yrs Hidden from public
Class 4–6 felony conv. Sealing 5 yrs Hidden from public
Class 2/3 felony conv. Sealing 10 yrs Hidden from public
Eligible conviction (immediate) Set-aside (§ 13-905) None Vacated, still visible
DUI conviction Set-aside / sealing Set-aside: none; MVD record persists
The effects differ sharply and the agent must state them precisely. Expungement (§ 36-2862) erases the
marijuana record. Sealing (§ 13-911) hides the record from public view and lets the person deny the
arrest/conviction in most contexts, while criminal-justice agencies and certain sensitive employers retain access.
Set-aside (§ 13-905) vacates the judgment of guilt and releases penalties/disabilities, but the conviction remains
publicly visible with a “set aside” annotation, can be used as a prior, and (except for a marijuana SALE sealed
under § 13-911(H), where the person must still disclose on applications) does not let the person deny it. None of
Be precise on effect: erased (marijuana), hidden-and-deniable (sealing), or vacated-but-visible
(set-aside). Don't describe a set-aside as making the record disappear.
For someone who wants the conviction truly out of public view, sealing is the goal; set-aside is the bridge
Only SEALING has class-based waits. Set-aside has none, and marijuana expungement has none. Sealing waits
Marijuana expungement § 36-2862 None
Set-aside (any eligible conviction) § 13-905 None (after discharge)
Sealing — dismissal / acquittal § 13-911 None (right away)
Sealing — Class 2 or 3 misdemeanor § 13-911 2 years
Sealing — Class 1 misdemeanor § 13-911 3 years
Sealing — Class 4, 5, or 6 felony § 13-911 5 years
Sealing — Class 2 or 3 felony § 13-911 10 years
Sealing — subsequent felony § 13-911 Class wait + 5 additional years
Two timing points matter for sealing. First, the clock starts when the person completes the NONMONETARY
conditions of probation or the sentence and is discharged — but the person must also have paid all fines, fees,
period PLUS five more years before sealing it. Set-aside, by contrast, can be sought as soon as the person is
discharged, which is why it is the immediate-relief option while a sealing wait runs.
Apply the sealing tiers by class: 2 yrs (Class 2/3 misd), 3 yrs (Class 1 misd), 5 yrs (Class 4–6 felony), 10
yrs (Class 2/3 felony); dismissals/acquittals can seal immediately.
Confirm all fines/fees/restitution are paid before counting a sealing wait as satisfied — and offer set-aside
Certain serious driving/traffic offenses (note: SB 1249 (2021) removed many traffic offenses from the set-aside exclusion).
Marijuana expungement (§ 36-2862) scope limits
DUI and MVD records persist. A set-aside or sealing does not rewrite ADOT/MVD driving history; DUI priors remain usable for enhancement.
Out-of-state / federal convictions are not reachable by Arizona's remedies.
The exclusion lists are defined by cross-referenced statutes (§§ 13-105, 13-705, 13-706, 13-3821,
13-118) and the sealing law is recent (eff. 1/1/2023) and still being interpreted; offense-class
mapping is decisive. The agent must classify the Arizona offense, screen it against the correct
remedy's exclusions, and verify the current statute before declaring eligibility.
Class 1 felonies are out for sealing; the set-aside list overlaps but adds victim-under-15.
Note the DUI/MVD carve-out every time — even a granted set-aside or sealing leaves ADOT records
Remedy 1 — Record sealing (A.R.S. § 13-911)
Remedy 2 — Set-aside of a conviction (A.R.S. § 13-905)
Remedy 3 — Marijuana expungement (A.R.S. § 36-2862, Prop 207)
No source-defined jurisdiction pathway matched the submitted facts.

AGENT GUIDANCE Apply the sealing tiers by class: 2 yrs (Class 2/3 misd), 3 yrs (Class 1 misd), 5 yrs (Class 4–6 felony), 10 yrs (Class 2/3 felony); dismissals/acquittals can seal immediately.

exclusionRules.ruleText: Each remedy has its own exclusion list. Serious, dangerous, and sexual offenses are broadly excluded from both
Not eligible for SEALING (§ 13-911(O))
A dangerous offense as defined in § 13-105.
A dangerous crime against children as defined in § 13-705.
A serious offense or violent or aggravated felony as defined in § 13-706.
Any offense with an element of the discharge, use, or threatening exhibition of a deadly weapon or dangerous instrument, or the knowing infliction of serious physical injury.
Any Class 1 felony (in Arizona, first- and second-degree murder).
Offenses requiring sex-offender registration (§ 13-3821) or with a sexual-motivation finding (§ 13-118).
Not eligible for SET-ASIDE (§ 13-905(K))
A dangerous offense; an offense requiring registration under § 13-3821; an offense with a sexual-motivation finding under § 13-118.
A felony in which the victim is a minor under fifteen.
Certain serious driving/traffic offenses (note: SB 1249 (2021) removed many traffic offenses from the set-aside exclusion).
An offense involving the infliction of serious physical injury or the use of a deadly weapon/dangerous instrument.
Marijuana expungement (§ 36-2862) scope limits

Only the Prop-207 conduct qualifies (≤ 2.5 oz / ≤ 6 plants / paraphernalia). A marijuana SALE offense is NOT expungeable under § 36-2862, though it may be SEALABLE under § 13-911 after the waiting period.
Limits that apply across remedies
DUI and MVD records persist. A set-aside or sealing does not rewrite ADOT/MVD driving history; DUI priors remain usable for enhancement.
Out-of-state / federal convictions are not reachable by Arizona's remedies.
Victim notification. Prosecutors and victims have statutory notice and input rights.
The exclusion lists are defined by cross-referenced statutes (§§ 13-105, 13-705, 13-706, 13-3821,
13-118) and the sealing law is recent (eff. 1/1/2023) and still being interpreted; offense-class
mapping is decisive. The agent must classify the Arizona offense, screen it against the correct
remedy's exclusions, and verify the current statute before declaring eligibility.
Arizona Record Relief — Agent Training Reference · LegalEase / Wilma
Screen against the remedy that's being used: dangerous (§ 13-105), serious/violent/aggravated (§
13-706), dangerous crime against children (§ 13-705), deadly-weapon or serious-injury element, and
Class 1 felonies are out for sealing; the set-aside list overlaps but adds victim-under-15.
Note the DUI/MVD carve-out every time — even a granted set-aside or sealing leaves ADOT records
Serious, dangerous, and sexual offenses are broadly excluded from both sealing and set-aside.

Not eligible for SEALING (§ 13-911(O)) • A dangerous offense as defined in § 13-105. • A dangerous crime against children as defined in § 13-705. • A serious offense or violent or aggravated felony as defined in § 13-706. • Any offense with an element of the discharge, use, or threatening exhibition of a deadly weapon or dangerous instrument, or the knowing infliction of serious physical injury. • Any Class 1 felony (in Arizona, first- and second-degree murder). • Offenses requiring sex-offender registration (§ 13-3821) or with a sexual-motivation finding (§ 13-118).

Not eligible for SET-ASIDE (§ 13-905(K)) • A dangerous offense; an offense requiring registration under § 13-3821; an offense with a sexual-motivation finding under § 13-118. • A felony in which the victim is a minor under fifteen. • Certain serious driving/traffic offenses (note: SB 1249 (2021) removed many traffic offenses from the set-aside exclusion). • An offense involving the infliction of serious physical injury or the use of a deadly weapon/dangerous instrument.

feeRules: Marijuana expungement (§ 36-2862) $0 Filing fees waived by statute
Record sealing (§ 13-911) Court filing fee Fee waiver may be available
Set-aside (§ 13-905) Court filing fee Fee waiver may be available
Certificate of Second Chance Included w/ set-aside No separate petition needed
DPS criminal history record DPS fee To confirm offenses and dates
Attorney fees (if represented) Varies Not required; legal-aid clinics assist
Cost is modest and the marijuana path is free. Expungement of a Prop-207 marijuana offense under § 36-2862
carries no filing fee. Sealing and set-aside petitions carry the court's ordinary filing fee, but a fee waiver

may be available for those who cannot afford it. The main other cost is obtaining the DPS criminal history to confirm the offenses, classes, and dates, plus any attorney fees if the person chooses representation. Because sealing requires all monetary obligations (fines, fees, restitution) to be satisfied first, resolving outstanding court debt is often the practical prerequisite rather than the filing fee itself.
Lead with the free marijuana path where it fits. For sealing, the real gate is paying off court debt and
restitution, not the filing fee — and a fee waiver may cover the filing cost.
Pull the DPS criminal history first so the offense classes (which drive the sealing waits) are confirmed.
Arizona Record Relief — Agent Training Reference · LegalEase / Wilma

Sealing and set-aside petitions carry the court's ordinary filing fee, but a fee waiver (deferral) may be available for those who cannot afford it.
The main other cost is obtaining the DPS criminal history to confirm the offenses, classes, and dates, plus any attorney fees if the person chooses representation.

generatorSelectionContract.failureBehavior: Return needs_more_info or needs_review. Never fall back to a generic packet or another jurisdiction generator.

packet_ready.title: You may have a packet-ready path.

packet_ready_with_caution.title: You may have a path, with cautions.

needs_more_info.title: We need a few more details.

guidance_only.title: We can give you state-specific next steps.

not_covered_yet.title: This record type is not supported by the current packet set.

likely_not_eligible.title: This record may not match a self-help filing path.

needs_review.title: This situation needs review before a packet is generated.

hard_stop.title: This tool cannot prepare a packet for this matter.

copyGuardrails: Use Arizona-specific remedy terminology returned by the profile; do not relabel every remedy as expungement.
Use “may qualify,” “possible path,” and “the court or agency makes the final decision.”
Do not promise erasure, sealing, eligibility, timing, filing acceptance, or a court outcome.
Do not show checkout unless the backend returns a packet-ready result and paymentAllowed=true.

sourceSections.title: Sample petitions and completed examples
sourceSections.text:
Statutory shorthand used throughout

A.R.S. § 13-911 Record SEALING of arrest, conviction, and sentencing records (eff. 1/1/2023)

A.R.S. § 13-905 SET-ASIDE of a conviction; release from penalties; Certificate of Second Chance

A.R.S. § 36-2862 EXPUNGEMENT of certain marijuana offenses (Proposition 207)

A.R.S. § 13-910 Restoration of civil rights and firearm rights

A.R.S. § 13-105 Defines DANGEROUS OFFENSE (sealing- and set-aside-excluded)

A.R.S. § 13-705 Defines DANGEROUS CRIME AGAINST CHILDREN (sealing-excluded)

A.R.S. § 13-706 Defines SERIOUS / violent / aggravated felony (sealing-excluded)

§ 13-3821 / § 13-118 Sex-offender registration / finding of sexual motivation (excluded)

Cert. of Second Chance Issued with many set-asides; eases occupational licensing

Arizona Record Relief — Agent Training Reference · LegalEase / Wilma

sourceSections.title: Eligibility rules (the three remedies)
sourceSections.text:
Three separate remedies, each with its own eligibility logic.

Remedy 1 — Record sealing (A.R.S. § 13-911)
The broadest modern relief, effective January 1, 2023:

• Convictions — a person convicted of an eligible offense may seal the arrest, conviction, and sentencing
records after completing all NONMONETARY terms of the sentence and the applicable waiting period (by
offense class).
• Dismissals / acquittals — a person whose charges were dismissed, who was acquitted, or whose charge was
reduced to a lesser eligible offense may petition to seal, generally right away.
• Effect — records are hidden from public view; the person may state in most contexts that the
arrest/conviction did not occur (with exceptions for certain sensitive positions/licenses). Law enforcement,
courts, and certain agencies still have access.
• Subsequent felony — a person who sealed records and later commits a felony may seal the later case only
after that offense's waiting period PLUS an additional five years.

Remedy 2 — Set-aside of a conviction (A.R.S. § 13-905)
• Available to most people convicted of a criminal offense, on fulfillment of the conditions of probation or
sentence and discharge by the court — no waiting period.
• Effect — the judgment of guilt is vacated and the person is released from penalties and disabilities, but the
record stays PUBLIC with a “set aside” annotation; the conviction can still be used in a later prosecution and
remains on background checks.
• Firearm rights are restored on set-aside except for a serious offense (§ 13-706).
• Certificate of Second Chance may be granted with the set-aside, easing occupational licensing.

Remedy 3 — Marijuana expungement (A.R.S. § 36-2862, Prop 207)
• True EXPUNGEMENT — the record is erased and accessible only to law enforcement/courts in limited
situations.
• Limited to Prop-207 marijuana conduct: possessing/consuming/transporting 2.5 ounces or less (≤ 12.5 g
concentrate); possessing/cultivating no more than six plants at a primary residence for personal use; and
associated paraphernalia.
• No waiting period, and filing fees are waived; applies to arrests, charges, adjudications, and convictions.

AGENT GUIDANCE
Check the marijuana path first — if the offense fits § 36-2862, expungement is the strongest, fastest, free
remedy. Otherwise test sealing (§ 13-911) and the waiting tier; use set-aside (§ 13-905) for immediate
relief or when sealing isn't available.
Remember the sequence strategy: many petitioners get a set-aside now and seal later once the waiting
period passes.

Arizona Record Relief — Agent Training Reference · LegalEase / Wilma

sourceSections.title: Waiting periods
sourceSections.text:
Only SEALING has class-based waits. Set-aside has none, and marijuana expungement has none. Sealing waits
run from completion of the NONMONETARY sentence conditions and discharge.

Remedy / offense level Statute Waiting period

Marijuana expungement § 36-2862 None

Set-aside (any eligible conviction) § 13-905 None (after discharge)

Sealing — dismissal / acquittal § 13-911 None (right away)

Sealing — Class 2 or 3 misdemeanor § 13-911 2 years

Sealing — Class 1 misdemeanor § 13-911 3 years

Sealing — Class 4, 5, or 6 felony § 13-911 5 years

Sealing — Class 2 or 3 felony § 13-911 10 years

Sealing — subsequent felony § 13-911 Class wait + 5 additional years

Two timing points matter for sealing. First, the clock starts when the person completes the NONMONETARY
conditions of probation or the sentence and is discharged — but the person must also have paid all fines, fees,
and restitution to actually qualify (outstanding restitution is a common bar). Second, the subsequent-felony rule
stacks: someone who sealed an earlier record and then commits a new felony must wait that new offense's class
period PLUS five more years before sealing it. Set-aside, by contrast, can be sought as soon as the person is
discharged, which is why it is the immediate-relief option while a sealing wait runs.

AGENT GUIDANCE
Apply the sealing tiers by class: 2 yrs (Class 2/3 misd), 3 yrs (Class 1 misd), 5 yrs (Class 4–6 felony), 10
yrs (Class 2/3 felony); dismissals/acquittals can seal immediately.
Confirm all fines/fees/restitution are paid before counting a sealing wait as satisfied — and offer set-aside
in the meantime, since it has no wait.

Arizona Record Relief — Agent Training Reference · LegalEase / Wilma

sourceSections.title: Disqualifying / excluded offenses
sourceSections.text:
Each remedy has its own exclusion list. Serious, dangerous, and sexual offenses are broadly excluded from both
sealing and set-aside.

Not eligible for SEALING (§ 13-911(O))
• A dangerous offense as defined in § 13-105.
• A dangerous crime against children as defined in § 13-705.
• A serious offense or violent or aggravated felony as defined in § 13-706.
• Any offense with an element of the discharge, use, or threatening exhibition of a deadly weapon or
dangerous instrument, or the knowing infliction of serious physical injury.
• Any Class 1 felony (in Arizona, first- and second-degree murder).
• Offenses requiring sex-offender registration (§ 13-3821) or with a sexual-motivation finding (§ 13-118).

Not eligible for SET-ASIDE (§ 13-905(K))
• A dangerous offense; an offense requiring registration under § 13-3821; an offense with a sexual-motivation
finding under § 13-118.
• A felony in which the victim is a minor under fifteen.
• Certain serious driving/traffic offenses (note: SB 1249 (2021) removed many traffic offenses from the
set-aside exclusion).
• An offense involving the infliction of serious physical injury or the use of a deadly weapon/dangerous
instrument.

Marijuana expungement (§ 36-2862) scope limits
• Only the Prop-207 conduct qualifies (≤ 2.5 oz / ≤ 6 plants / paraphernalia). A marijuana SALE offense is
NOT expungeable under § 36-2862, though it may be SEALABLE under § 13-911 after the waiting period.

Limits that apply across remedies
• DUI and MVD records persist. A set-aside or sealing does not rewrite ADOT/MVD driving history; DUI
priors remain usable for enhancement.
• Out-of-state / federal convictions are not reachable by Arizona's remedies.
• Victim notification. Prosecutors and victims have statutory notice and input rights.

VERIFY BEFORE RELYING
The exclusion lists are defined by cross-referenced statutes (§§ 13-105, 13-705, 13-706, 13-3821,
13-118) and the sealing law is recent (eff. 1/1/2023) and still being interpreted; offense-class
mapping is decisive. The agent must classify the Arizona offense, screen it against the correct
remedy's exclusions, and verify the current statute before declaring eligibility.

Arizona Record Relief — Agent Training Reference · LegalEase / Wilma

AGENT GUIDANCE
Screen against the remedy that's being used: dangerous (§ 13-105), serious/violent/aggravated (§
13-706), dangerous crime against children (§ 13-705), deadly-weapon or serious-injury element, and
Class 1 felonies are out for sealing; the set-aside list overlaps but adds victim-under-15.
Note the DUI/MVD carve-out every time — even a granted set-aside or sealing leaves ADOT records
and DUI priorability intact.

Arizona Record Relief — Agent Training Reference · LegalEase / Wilma

sourceSections.title: Case outcome rules
sourceSections.text:
Offense type and disposition drive which remedy applies. This table is the agent's primary routing logic for
Arizona.

Case outcome / offense Remedy Wait Effect

Marijuana ≤ 2.5 oz / ≤ 6 plants / Expungement None Erased
paraphernalia (§ 36-2862)

Dismissed / acquitted (eligible) Sealing (§ 13-911) None Hidden from public

Class 2/3 misdemeanor conv. Sealing 2 yrs Hidden from public

Class 1 misdemeanor conv. Sealing 3 yrs Hidden from public

Class 4–6 felony conv. Sealing 5 yrs Hidden from public

Class 2/3 felony conv. Sealing 10 yrs Hidden from public

Eligible conviction (immediate) Set-aside (§ 13-905) None Vacated, still visible

DUI conviction Set-aside / sealing Set-aside: none; MVD record persists
seal: by class

Dangerous / serious / sex / — Not eligible —
Class 1 felony

The effects differ sharply and the agent must state them precisely. Expungement (§ 36-2862) erases the
marijuana record. Sealing (§ 13-911) hides the record from public view and lets the person deny the
arrest/conviction in most contexts, while criminal-justice agencies and certain sensitive employers retain access.
Set-aside (§ 13-905) vacates the judgment of guilt and releases penalties/disabilities, but the conviction remains
publicly visible with a “set aside” annotation, can be used as a prior, and (except for a marijuana SALE sealed
under § 13-911(H), where the person must still disclose on applications) does not let the person deny it. None of
the three rewrites ADOT/MVD records.

AGENT GUIDANCE
Be precise on effect: erased (marijuana), hidden-and-deniable (sealing), or vacated-but-visible
(set-aside). Don't describe a set-aside as making the record disappear.
For someone who wants the conviction truly out of public view, sealing is the goal; set-aside is the bridge
until the sealing wait passes.

Arizona Record Relief — Agent Training Reference · LegalEase / Wilma

sourceSections.title: Required forms
sourceSections.text:
Arizona uses Judicial Branch / Supreme Court (AOC) petition forms filed in the court that handled the case
(superior, justice, or municipal). Each remedy has its own petition and a companion order. The current form
numbers are below.

Form no. Form / filing Use

AOCCRSL1F-091424 Petition to Seal Criminal Case Seal arrest, charging, and conviction records. Companion
Records (§ 13-911) 5-page instructions AOCCRSL1I-091424. Shows
nonmonetary conditions complete, all monetary obligations
paid, and the class waiting period elapsed; served on the
prosecutor.

AOCCRSL2F-091424 Order Regarding Petition to Seal Proposed order for the judge: grants, denies, or dismisses;
(§ 13-911) on grant directs the clerk and DPS to seal and notifies the
prosecutor. 3-year refile bar after a denial.

AOC CR41FORM31A-0 Application to Set Aside Vacates the judgment of guilt; may request a Certificate of
10120 Conviction (§ 13-905) Second Chance and firearm-rights restoration. Five
sections plus a self / authorization signature fork.

AOC CR41FORM31B-0 Order re Set Aside & Restoration Proposed order carrying the Certificate of Second Chance
92921 of Firearm Rights (§§ 13-905 & and firearm-restoration findings. Note: the order revision
13-910) (092921) is newer than the application (010120).

AOC CREM2F-071221 Petition to Expunge Prop-207 conduct only (≤ 2.5 oz / ≤ 6 plants /
Marijuana-Related Offense paraphernalia). No fee, no waiting period. Dual petitioner /
Records (§ 36-2862) attorney signature blocks.

Each filing is made in the court of conviction or charge and identifies the case number, the offense and class,
the disposition, and the statutory basis. The sealing petition must show the nonmonetary conditions are
complete and the waiting period has passed; the set-aside application shows discharge from probation or
sentence; the marijuana expungement petition shows the offense fits § 36-2862. Prosecutors and victims
receive notice and may respond, and the court may hold a hearing before ruling.

AGENT GUIDANCE
Cite the form by number: sealing is AOCCRSL1F-091424 (with order AOCCRSL2F); set-aside is AOC CR41FORM31A
with order CR41FORM31B; marijuana expungement is AOC CREM2F. File in the court that handled the case.
On the set-aside application, request the Certificate of Second Chance and firearm-rights restoration where eligible — and
note the application form (010120) is a revision behind its own order (092921), so confirm the current version at
azcourts.gov before filing.

Arizona Record Relief — Agent Training Reference · LegalEase / Wilma

sourceSections.title: Filing instructions
sourceSections.text:
1 Get the record. Pull the Arizona DPS criminal history (and the court docket) to confirm each charge, the
offense class, the disposition, and the sentence-completion/discharge dates.

2 Pick the remedy. Marijuana within Prop 207 → § 36-2862 expungement; eligible conviction with the wait
satisfied → § 13-911 sealing; eligible conviction for immediate relief or sealing-ineligible → § 13-905
set-aside. Screen the exclusions for the chosen remedy.

3 Confirm prerequisites. For sealing, all nonmonetary conditions complete, all fines/fees/restitution paid, and
the class waiting period elapsed. For set-aside, discharge from probation/sentence. For marijuana, that the
offense fits § 36-2862.

4 Complete the petition and proposed order for the chosen remedy, citing the statute, the case number, and
the offense/class.
5 File in the court that handled the case (superior, justice, or municipal). Marijuana expungement filing fees
are waived; sealing/set-aside may carry a fee or fee waiver.

6 Notice to the prosecutor and victim. Serve the prosecutor; victims have statutory notice and input rights —
do not skip this, as it risks denial or delay.

7 Hearing / ruling. The court may rule on the petition with or without a hearing after any prosecutor/victim
response; if granted, the order directs sealing, set-aside, or expungement.

AGENT GUIDANCE
File in the court of conviction/charge. Marijuana expungement is free; for sealing, make sure every
monetary obligation is satisfied before filing.
Always handle prosecutor service and victim notice — missing the victim-notice step is a common cause
of denial or delay in Arizona.

Arizona Record Relief — Agent Training Reference · LegalEase / Wilma

sourceSections.title: Court-specific quirks
sourceSections.text:
• Three remedies, three effects. Expungement (marijuana, erased), sealing (hidden), set-aside (vacated but
visible). Using the wrong word with a client causes real confusion.
• Sealing is new (1/1/2023). Created by SB 1294 (2021); pre-2023 guidance that says Arizona has “no
expungement” is now incomplete.
• Set-aside leaves an annotation. The conviction still appears, marked “set aside” — it is not hidden and can
be a prior.
• Sequence strategy. Set-aside now (no wait) for immediate benefit, then seal once the class waiting period
passes.
• Marijuana sale is different. Not expungeable under § 36-2862, but may be sealable under § 13-911 — and a
sealed sale still requires disclosure on applications under § 13-911(H).
• Firearm rights. Set-aside restores firearm rights except for serious offenses (§ 13-706); see also § 13-910
restoration.
• DUI / MVD persist. ADOT driving records and DUI priorability survive any of these remedies.
• Subsequent-felony stacking. Sealing a later felony after an earlier sealing requires the class wait plus five
more years.
• Certificate of Second Chance. Often issued with a set-aside; helps with occupational licensing barriers.

AGENT GUIDANCE
The most common Arizona mistake is conflating the three remedies — always name which one and what
it does. Lead with marijuana expungement if it fits, then sealing, then set-aside.
Flag the DUI/MVD carve-out and the marijuana-sale nuance proactively; both surprise people.

Arizona Record Relief — Agent Training Reference · LegalEase / Wilma

sourceSections.title: Fee information
sourceSections.text:
Item Amount Note

Marijuana expungement (§ 36-2862) $0 Filing fees waived by statute

Record sealing (§ 13-911) Court filing fee Fee waiver may be available

Set-aside (§ 13-905) Court filing fee Fee waiver may be available

Certificate of Second Chance Included w/ set-aside No separate petition needed

DPS criminal history record DPS fee To confirm offenses and dates

Attorney fees (if represented) Varies Not required; legal-aid clinics assist

Cost is modest and the marijuana path is free. Expungement of a Prop-207 marijuana offense under § 36-2862
carries no filing fee. Sealing and set-aside petitions carry the court's ordinary filing fee, but a fee waiver
(deferral) may be available for those who cannot afford it. The main other cost is obtaining the DPS criminal
history to confirm the offenses, classes, and dates, plus any attorney fees if the person chooses representation.
Because sealing requires all monetary obligations (fines, fees, restitution) to be satisfied first, resolving
outstanding court debt is often the practical prerequisite rather than the filing fee itself.

AGENT GUIDANCE
Lead with the free marijuana path where it fits. For sealing, the real gate is paying off court debt and
restitution, not the filing fee — and a fee waiver may cover the filing cost.
Pull the DPS criminal history first so the offense classes (which drive the sealing waits) are confirmed.

Arizona Record Relief — Agent Training Reference · LegalEase / Wilma

sourceSections.title: Criminal history (DPS) request process
sourceSections.text:
Confirming the record establishes the offense class (which decides the sealing wait), the disposition, and the
discharge dates.

sourceSections.title: Sample petitions and completed examples
sourceSections.text:
Arizona filings go to the court that handled the case. The examples below show structure and key recitals.
Names, dates, and identifiers are fictional and illustrative.

Example A — Petition to Seal Records (A.R.S. § 13-911)
SUPERIOR COURT OF ARIZONA, MARICOPA COUNTY
STATE OF ARIZONA v. ANDRE L. CARTER No. CR2018-001234

PETITION TO SEAL CASE RECORDS (A.R.S. § 13-911)
1. Petitioner: Andre L. Carter; DOB 07/22/1992.
2. On 03/14/2019 petitioner was convicted of Theft, a Class 6
felony (A.R.S. § 13-1802), in this case.

sourceSections.title: The offense is eligible for sealing (not a dangerous offense,
sourceSections.text:
dangerous crime against children, serious/violent/aggravated
felony, deadly-weapon or serious-injury offense, or Class 1
felony).
4. Petitioner completed all NONMONETARY conditions and was
discharged on 04/01/2020, and has paid all fines, fees, and
restitution.
5. More than FIVE years have passed (Class 4-6 felony tier).
6. Petitioner requests an order sealing the arrest, conviction,
and sentencing records under § 13-911. (Prosecutor served;
victim notified.)

Examples B & C — Set-aside and marijuana expungement

sourceSections.title: SET-ASIDE (A.R.S. § 13-905):
sourceSections.text:
'Having fulfilled the conditions of probation/sentence and
been discharged, petitioner applies to SET ASIDE the judgment
of guilt, to be released from penalties and disabilities, for
a CERTIFICATE OF SECOND CHANCE, and for restoration of firearm
rights (offense not a serious offense under § 13-706).'
NOTE: the conviction remains visible, annotated 'set aside.'

sourceSections.title: MARIJUANA EXPUNGEMENT (A.R.S. § 36-2862, Prop 207):
sourceSections.text:
'Petitioner was arrested/charged/convicted of possessing 2.5
ounces or less of marijuana (§ 13-3405/Prop 207 conduct).
Petitioner requests EXPUNGEMENT under § 36-2862.' No waiting
period; filing fee waived; the record is erased on grant.

What each order does
A § 13-911 sealing order directs the court, DPS, and agencies to seal the arrest, conviction, and sentencing
records, after which the person may deny the matter in most contexts. A § 13-905 set-aside order vacates the
judgment of guilt and releases penalties (and may grant a Certificate of Second Chance and restore firearm
rights), but the record stays public with a “set aside” note. A § 36-2862 expungement order erases the marijuana
record. None of the three rewrites ADOT/MVD driving records.

Arizona Record Relief — Agent Training Reference · LegalEase / Wilma

This reference is an internal training and retrieval aid for the Wilma assistant. It summarizes Arizona's three record-relief remedies
only for certain marijuana offenses (§ 36-2862, Prop 207); each has distinct eligibility, waits, and effects, and DUI/MVD records
persist. The agent must verify the current text of A.R.S. §§ 13-911, 13-905, 13-910, and 36-2862, the exclusion definitions, the
petition forms, DPS processes, and current fees before relying on any rule here.

Arizona Record Relief — Agent Training Reference · LegalEase / Wilma
